heroOverview: Overweight is defined by the World Health Organization as a body mass index (BMI) of 25.0–29.9 kg/m² in adults, reflecting excess body fat that elevates the risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, sleep apnoea, joint disease, and several cancers even before the threshold for obesity is reached. Globally, an estimated 2.5 billion adults were classified as overweight or obese in 2022, representing a near tripling of prevalence since 1975. The ICD-10 code E66.3 distinguishes overweight specifically from obesity, recognising that even this intermediate degree of excess weight carries measurable metabolic and cardiovascular risk. The central driver is a sustained positive energy balance — energy intake chronically exceeding energy expenditure — shaped by genetic predisposition, built environment, ultra-processed food availability, sedentary behaviour, sleep disruption, stress hormones, and economic factors.

keyStats.prevalence: 39% of adults worldwide were overweight (BMI 25–29.9) in 2022 per WHO; in the United States, approximately 31% of adults fall in the overweight-only category (CDC, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Affects adults of all ages; rates rise with age and plateau after 65; men have higher overweight prevalence in most high-income countries, women in many lower-income settings
keyStats.avgAge: Overweight prevalence increases progressively from young adulthood through middle age
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 1.9 billion adults worldwide classified as overweight (BMI 25–29.9) in 2022 (WHO)

definition: Overweight (ICD-10: E66.3) describes a body weight that exceeds a healthy range but falls short of the clinical obesity threshold, conventionally defined as a BMI of 25.0–29.9 kg/m² for adults. BMI is calculated as weight in kilograms divided by height in metres squared and is the most widely used population-level screening measure, though it does not directly measure body fat or its distribution. Waist circumference and waist-to-hip ratio provide complementary information about central adiposity — fat deposited around the abdominal organs — which more specifically predicts cardiometabolic risk than overall BMI alone. The metabolic risk of overweight varies by ethnicity: Asian populations experience elevated type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular risk at BMIs of 23–24.9, prompting some guidelines to apply lower diagnostic thresholds. Overweight in childhood (BMI-for-age above the 85th percentile) is classified separately but tracks into adult overweight and obesity in approximately 50–80% of overweight adolescents. The condition represents a continuum from healthy weight to obesity rather than a discrete disease state, and the purpose of the ICD classification is to flag clinically meaningful excess weight that warrants preventive intervention before metabolic consequences become established.

typesClassification.description: The standard WHO adult overweight category; carries elevated risk of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and cardiovascular events compared with a BMI of 18.5–24.9, with risk increasing progressively through the range.

Excess adiposity concentrated in the abdomen as measured by waist circumference (above 88 cm in women, above 102 cm in men by NCEP/ATP III criteria); visceral fat is metabolically more active and more tightly linked to insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk than peripheral fat.
BMI-for-age at or above the 85th and below the 95th percentile; tracks strongly into adult overweight and requires family-based lifestyle intervention rather than weight-loss medications.

The combination of overweight with at least three of: elevated waist circumference, raised triglycerides, low HDL cholesterol, high blood pressure, or raised fasting glucose; this phenotype carries substantially higher cardiovascular risk than the BMI figure alone suggests.

primarySymptoms: Increased body weight and visible fat accumulation, particularly around the abdomen, hips, thighs, and upper arms, representing the primary and defining physical feature.
Reduced exercise tolerance and breathlessness on exertion that is disproportionate to apparent fitness, reflecting the increased cardiorespiratory workload of carrying excess mass.
Daytime fatigue and low energy, partly from disrupted sleep, insulin resistance, and the low-grade systemic inflammation associated with adipose tissue.
Snoring and witnessed breathing pauses during sleep from upper airway narrowing by pharyngeal fat, an early sign of obstructive sleep apnoea that worsens with increasing weight.
Joint pain — particularly in the knees, hips, and lower back — from the increased mechanical load placed on weight-bearing joints and altered biomechanics.
Increased sweating and heat intolerance from the insulating effect of subcutaneous fat and the metabolic heat generated by excess tissue.
Skin fold irritation, chafing, or intertrigo (moist, inflamed skin between folds) from friction in areas of fat accumulation.
Menstrual irregularity in women, caused by the disruption of oestrogen metabolism and insulin resistance associated with central adiposity.
Elevated blood pressure detected on routine measurement, often asymptomatic in the overweight range but representing a key early cardiovascular risk driver.
Fasting hyperglycaemia or impaired glucose tolerance — frequently asymptomatic at the overweight stage — detected on routine screening.
earlyWarningSigns: Waist circumference that has increased to or beyond the threshold for central obesity even before total BMI crosses 25.
Fasting blood glucose approaching the pre-diabetes range (5.6–6.9 mmol/L or 100–125 mg/dL) on a routine blood test.
A pattern of progressive, unintentional weight gain of 1–2 kg per year over several years without dietary or lifestyle change.
Worsening snoring or partner-witnessed breath-holding episodes during sleep.
emergencySigns: Chest pain, shortness of breath, or left arm discomfort in a person with overweight-associated cardiovascular risk factors, requiring immediate emergency assessment for acute coronary syndrome.
Sudden-onset severe headache, facial drooping, arm weakness, or speech difficulty indicating a possible stroke, for which BMI-associated hypertension and dyslipidaemia are risk factors.
Acute metabolic decompensation in a person with previously undiagnosed type 2 diabetes: extreme thirst, polyuria, and confusion.
Signs of pulmonary embolism — sudden breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis — to which excess weight significantly elevates risk.

causes.description: The proximate cause of overweight is a chronic excess of energy intake over energy expenditure; modern food environments, characterised by low cost, high palatability, and energy-dense ultra-processed foods, make a caloric surplus easy to accumulate — even as little as 100–200 kcal/day in excess produces a 5–10 kg weight gain over several years.

causes.cause: Sedentary behaviour and reduced non-exercise activity
causes.description: Office-based work, prolonged screen time, and motorised transport have dramatically reduced daily energy expenditure; non-exercise activity thermogenesis (NEAT) — the calories burned in fidgeting, standing, and incidental movement — is markedly lower in people who gain weight and may account for 300–500 kcal/day of inter-individual variability.

Twin studies estimate 40–70% heritability of BMI; genome-wide association studies have identified over 900 loci associated with BMI, influencing hypothalamic appetite regulation, adipocyte differentiation, and energy metabolism; genetic risk does not determine outcome but raises susceptibility in obesogenic environments.

Consistently less than 7 hours of sleep per night increases circulating ghrelin (appetite-stimulating) and decreases leptin (satiety-signalling), raising daily caloric intake by approximately 300–500 kcal in experimental studies; shift work and social jet lag compound this effect through circadian disruption of insulin secretion.

Chronic psychosocial stress activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, raising cortisol and promoting preferential deposition of visceral fat; stress and negative affect trigger reward-based eating of energy-dense foods through dopaminergic pathways, independently of physiological hunger.

Several commonly prescribed drug classes cause clinically significant weight gain: antipsychotics (olanzapine, clozapine), insulin and sulphonylureas, corticosteroids, some antidepressants (mirtazapine, tricyclics), anticonvulsants (valproate, gabapentin), and hormonal contraceptives; awareness of this enables proactive dietary and monitoring support.

Hypothyroidism, Cushing syndrome, polycystic ovary syndrome, hypothalamic damage, and growth hormone deficiency all promote weight gain through hormonal mechanisms; these secondary causes account for fewer than 5% of overweight cases but should be excluded when weight gain is rapid, associated with specific symptoms, or resistant to lifestyle intervention.

riskFactors.factor: High dietary intake of ultra-processed foods
riskFactors.description: Ultra-processed foods account for 50–60% of caloric intake in many high-income countries; they are engineered for high palatability, are energy-dense, nutrient-poor, low in fibre, and strongly associated with overweight and obesity in prospective cohort studies.

Adults who fail to meet the WHO recommendation of 150–300 minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week are at substantially higher risk of progressive weight gain; even light activity such as walking 7,500–10,000 steps daily reduces this risk.

A family history of overweight or obesity in both parents raises an individual's lifetime risk of overweight roughly fourfold compared with those with normal-weight parents, reflecting shared genetic loci governing appetite, metabolism, and fat distribution.

Habitual sleep of fewer than 7 hours per night is independently associated with a 35–55% higher risk of overweight and obesity in epidemiological studies; improving sleep hygiene is now a recognised target in weight management guidelines.

Income inequality, food insecurity, limited access to recreational facilities, and reliance on energy-dense cheap foods all increase overweight risk; the reverse socioeconomic gradient — in which lower-income groups have higher obesity rates — is characteristic of high-income countries.

Patients on antipsychotics, insulin, corticosteroids, and certain anticonvulsants are at substantially elevated risk; switching to weight-neutral alternatives where clinically appropriate is a valid preventive strategy.

Stopping smoking removes nicotine's appetite-suppressing and metabolic-boosting effects; average weight gain of 4–5 kg in the first year after cessation is common and may push borderline individuals into the overweight range.

affectedDemographics: Middle-aged adults aged 40–65 in both high-income and many middle-income countries
Children and adolescents in food-insecure environments with high availability of ultra-processed foods
Adults with sedentary occupations and limited leisure time for physical activity
People on long-term antipsychotic, corticosteroid, or insulin therapy
Individuals who have recently quit smoking without dietary management
Adults from lower socioeconomic backgrounds in high-income countries
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of overweight is straightforward: BMI is calculated from a calibrated weight and height measurement and compared against the WHO threshold of 25.0 kg/m². However, BMI measurement alone is insufficient for clinical management — the assessment must also quantify cardiometabolic risk. Waist circumference is measured at the midpoint between the bottom of the ribs and the iliac crest and compared against sex- and ethnicity-specific risk thresholds. Blood pressure is taken to identify hypertension. Fasting blood glucose and HbA1c screen for pre-diabetes and type 2 diabetes; a fasting lipid panel detects dyslipidaemia. Liver function tests and, when indicated, liver ultrasound identify non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. Thyroid function (TSH) is checked to exclude hypothyroidism as a secondary cause. A sleep history and, if warranted, overnight oximetry or formal polysomnography screens for obstructive sleep apnoea. Psychiatric assessment for binge eating disorder, depression, and anxiety is appropriate because these conditions both cause and complicate weight management. In children and adolescents, BMI-for-age charts are used; causes of secondary obesity (Cushing syndrome, hypothyroidism, pseudohypoparathyroidism, syndromic obesity) are considered when weight gain is rapid or associated with short stature or dysmorphic features.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes the overweight classification (BMI 25.0–29.9); a standardised starting point for all weight management assessments.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Calibrated scales for weight and a wall-mounted measuring tool for height; takes under 2 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies central adiposity, which more specifically predicts metabolic and cardiovascular risk than BMI; elevated waist circumference (above 80 cm in women, 94 cm in men by European standards) identifies higher-risk overweight individuals.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible tape placed around the midriff midway between the last rib and the top of the hip bone; takes under one minute.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for pre-diabetes (glucose 5.6–6.9 mmol/L; HbA1c 39–47 mmol/mol) and type 2 diabetes that frequently accompanies overweight.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw after an overnight fast of at least 8 hours; results within a few hours to one day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies dyslipidaemia — elevated LDL and triglycerides, low HDL — which often develops alongside central adiposity and significantly raises cardiovascular risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw after an overnight fast; reported as total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, and triglycerides.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hypertension, which occurs in approximately 50% of overweight adults and substantially amplifies cardiovascular risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An automated or manual cuff measurement taken seated after 5 minutes rest; repeated on at least two separate occasions to confirm.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hypothyroidism as a secondary cause of weight gain; particularly relevant when weight gain is recent, rapid, or accompanied by cold intolerance, constipation, or fatigue.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver ultrasound and liver function tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (metabolic-associated steatotic liver disease), which affects approximately 50–75% of obese and overweight adults and can progress to cirrhosis if untreated.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood tests for ALT, AST, and GGT; ultrasound is a painless 15–20 minute abdominal scan.

treatmentOverview: Sustainable weight management in overweight rests on three pillars: dietary modification, physical activity, and behavioural support. Clinical guidelines from NICE, AHA/ACC, and the European Association for the Study of Obesity recommend targeting a 5–10% reduction in body weight over 3–6 months — a modest loss that consistently reduces blood pressure, improves glycaemia, lowers triglycerides, and decreases the risk of progressing to type 2 diabetes by approximately 50%. Dietary strategies with evidence include a moderately reduced-calorie eating pattern (500 kcal deficit per day), Mediterranean-style diets, low-carbohydrate approaches, and time-restricted eating; the most effective diet is one the individual can sustain. Physical activity targets 150–300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week, with resistance training at least two days per week to preserve muscle mass during weight loss. Structured behavioural interventions — cognitive behavioural approaches addressing eating triggers, portion awareness, and self-monitoring — significantly improve outcomes and are central to all evidence-based programmes. For individuals in the overweight BMI range who have not responded to lifestyle measures and who carry significant metabolic risk (pre-diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, or obstructive sleep apnoea), pharmacotherapy may be considered; approved agents include GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide), naltrexone-bupropion, and orlistat, with GLP-1 agonists producing the largest average weight loss of 10–15% of body weight in trials. Bariatric surgery is typically reserved for BMI above 35 with comorbidities or above 40, not for the overweight range alone. Mental health treatment for binge eating disorder or depression is an integral component when these coexist, as untreated psychiatric comorbidities substantially undermine weight loss efforts.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured intensive lifestyle intervention
medicalTreatments.description: The evidence-based first-line treatment: a combination of individualised dietary counselling, physical activity prescription, and behavioural support delivered by a dietitian, physiotherapist, and/or psychologist in group or individual sessions; programmes lasting 12+ months are significantly more effective than brief advice.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves 5–10% body weight reduction in approximately 60–70% of motivated participants in 12-month programmes; reduces type 2 diabetes incidence by approximately 58% in pre-diabetic individuals (DPP trial).

medicalTreatments.name: Semaglutide (GLP-1 receptor agonist)
medicalTreatments.description: A once-weekly injectable GLP-1 receptor agonist approved for weight management; reduces appetite, delays gastric emptying, and promotes satiety through central and peripheral mechanisms. Common side effects are nausea and GI upset, especially at initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Average body weight reduction of approximately 12–15% over 68 weeks in the STEP trials; also reduces cardiovascular events by approximately 20% in high-risk individuals (SELECT trial).

medicalTreatments.name: Liraglutide (GLP-1 receptor agonist)
medicalTreatments.description: A once-daily injectable GLP-1 receptor agonist approved at 3 mg for weight management; mechanism is similar to semaglutide with a shorter duration of action.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Average 6–8% body weight reduction versus 2–3% with placebo over 56 weeks in the SCALE trial; also reduces type 2 diabetes progression.

medicalTreatments.name: Orlistat
medicalTreatments.description: A pancreatic lipase inhibitor taken three times daily with meals; blocks absorption of approximately 30% of dietary fat, producing oily stools and faecal urgency — side effects that motivate lower-fat food choices. Available over the counter in some countries at lower doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Average 3–4% greater weight loss than placebo at 12 months; reduces type 2 diabetes incidence by approximately 37% in overweight individuals with impaired glucose tolerance.

medicalTreatments.name: Naltrexone-bupropion (Contrave)
medicalTreatments.description: A combination oral tablet combining a dopamine-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor with an opioid antagonist; acts on hypothalamic and reward pathways to reduce appetite and cravings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Average 5–6% greater weight loss than placebo over 56 weeks; not indicated in those with seizure disorders or current opioid use.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mediterranean dietary pattern
alternativeTreatments.description: A dietary approach emphasising vegetables, whole grains, legumes, fish, olive oil, and moderate wine with reduced ultra-processed food intake; associated with 5–7% weight reduction in randomised trials and cardiovascular risk reduction independent of weight loss.

alternativeTreatments.name: Structured walking programmes and wearable activity monitoring
alternativeTreatments.description: Daily step-count targets tracked with a pedometer or smartphone app are effective at increasing physical activity in overweight adults; programmes targeting 7,000–10,000 steps daily produce modest but clinically meaningful weight loss and improved cardiometabolic markers over 6–12 months.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sleep improvement interventions
alternativeTreatments.description: Sleep extension in habitually short sleepers (less than 6.5 hours) reduces daily caloric intake by approximately 270 kcal in randomised controlled studies; addressing obstructive sleep apnoea with CPAP improves weight management adherence and metabolic outcomes.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist or obesity medicine specialist is warranted when overweight is complicated by pre-diabetes, dyslipidaemia, hypertension, or polycystic ovary syndrome; when a secondary hormonal cause is suspected; when pharmacotherapy is being considered; or when the patient has made multiple unsuccessful lifestyle attempts. A registered dietitian is the core professional for dietary intervention. A psychologist or psychiatrist addresses binge eating disorder, depression, and trauma-related eating.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a clinician trained in obesity medicine or a multidisciplinary weight management programme that includes dietary, physical activity, and psychological components together, not a single-discipline approach. Ask whether the programme offers long-term follow-up of at least 12–24 months, as short programmes produce weight regain. For patients considering pharmacotherapy, a specialist familiar with GLP-1 receptor agonists and their cardiovascular benefit profiles is valuable.
preventionStrategies: Maintain regular physical activity meeting WHO guidelines: at least 150–300 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise per week, plus two sessions of resistance training.
Limit ultra-processed food and sugar-sweetened beverage consumption; these are the most consistent dietary risk factors for overweight in prospective studies.
Prioritise 7–9 hours of sleep per night; address obstructive sleep apnoea promptly.
Monitor waist circumference and weight annually from age 30, even if BMI is normal, to detect early abdominal adiposity.
When starting weight-promoting medications, proactively implement dietary supervision and monitoring protocols to limit weight gain.
Support breastfeeding and healthy complementary feeding practices in infants and children, which reduce the risk of childhood and adult overweight.
lifestyleModifications: Reduce portion sizes through smaller plates, measuring key foods, and not eating directly from packages.
Replace sugar-sweetened beverages with water; even this single change produces measurable weight loss over 12 months.
Increase incidental activity: walk instead of driving for short distances, use stairs, stand at a desk for part of the working day.
Eat breakfast, avoid skipping meals, and plan meals in advance to reduce impulsive food choices.
Limit alcohol, which adds substantial calories without nutritional value and impairs appetite regulation.
Practice consistent meal timing aligned with daytime hours to support circadian metabolic rhythms.

recommended: High-vegetable, high-fibre diets (at least 30 g fibre per day) to improve satiety and support gut microbiome diversity
Lean protein at each meal (fish, legumes, poultry, eggs) to preserve muscle mass and increase satiety per calorie
Whole grains (oats, barley, wholegrain bread) over refined carbohydrates to reduce glycaemic excursions
Olive oil, nuts, and avocado as primary fat sources; the Mediterranean pattern has the strongest evidence for cardiometabolic benefit
avoid: Sugar-sweetened drinks including fruit juice, which deliver large caloric loads without satiety and strongly predict weight gain
Ultra-processed snack foods, fast food, and confectionery — the single largest driver of excess caloric intake in population studies
Large alcohol intake, which provides 7 kcal per gram, impairs fat oxidation, and stimulates appetite
Skipping meals, particularly breakfast, which is associated with compensatory overeating and poor food choices later in the day

exerciseGuidelines: WHO guidelines recommend at least 150–300 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity (brisk walking, cycling, swimming) per week, or 75–150 minutes of vigorous activity, for cardiometabolic health and weight management. For weight loss specifically, 225–300 minutes per week of moderate activity has stronger evidence for producing and sustaining clinically meaningful loss. Resistance training two to three days per week preserves lean muscle mass during caloric restriction and improves insulin sensitivity. For overweight beginners, starting with 20–30-minute walking sessions and progressively building intensity reduces injury risk and improves adherence. Even 5–10% weight reduction combined with regular physical activity is sufficient to substantially reduce the risk of progressing to type 2 diabetes.
dailyManagement: Self-monitor weight once weekly rather than daily to track trends without being discouraged by normal day-to-day fluctuations.
Use a food diary or smartphone app for at least 12 weeks to increase awareness of actual caloric intake versus estimated intake.
Schedule physical activity in the calendar as a fixed appointment, not a flexible 'if I have time' option.
Review medications with a prescribing clinician if any current drugs are contributing to weight gain.
Attend all follow-up appointments with the dietitian and weight management team; consistent contact over 12 months doubles long-term weight-loss maintenance.
prognosis: The prognosis for overweight is variable and depends primarily on whether metabolic consequences have developed and whether they are treated. A 5–10% weight reduction — achievable with lifestyle intervention — reliably reduces blood pressure by 3–8 mmHg, triglycerides by 10–20%, fasting glucose by 3–8%, and 10-year cardiovascular risk measurably. The Diabetes Prevention Program showed that a lifestyle programme producing 7% weight loss reduced type 2 diabetes incidence by 58% over 3 years, outperforming metformin alone. Long-term maintenance of weight loss is challenging; approximately 20–30% of people who lose 5% of body weight at 12 months maintain this at 5 years without ongoing support. With pharmacotherapy (GLP-1 agonists), sustained weight loss is maintained in a larger proportion, and cardiovascular outcomes data now support their use in high-risk individuals. Without intervention, overweight commonly progresses to obesity over 5–10 years, with escalating metabolic risk.
recoveryTimeline: Measurable cardiometabolic improvements begin within 2–4 weeks of a 5% caloric deficit; blood pressure starts falling within 4–8 weeks, and fasting glucose improves within 2–4 weeks of caloric restriction in pre-diabetic individuals. A 5% weight loss is typically achievable within 3–4 months with consistent adherence to a 500 kcal/day deficit. Sustained 10% loss takes 6–12 months for most people. Ongoing weight maintenance is the most important and most challenging phase; evidence supports continuing structured follow-up, self-monitoring, and in many cases pharmacotherapy for at least 2 years to prevent regain.
complications: Type 2 diabetes — the risk doubles for every 5-unit increase in BMI above 22; an estimated 80–90% of type 2 diabetes cases are attributable to overweight and obesity combined.

Cardiovascular disease — overweight raises blood pressure, LDL cholesterol, and triglycerides while lowering HDL, collectively increasing 10-year coronary artery disease risk by approximately 20–30% compared with a healthy BMI.

Obstructive sleep apnoea — upper airway fat narrows the pharynx during sleep; roughly 30–40% of overweight adults have at least mild OSA, causing fragmented sleep, daytime somnolence, and further weight gain.

Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (metabolic-associated steatotic liver disease) — present in approximately 50–75% of obese and overweight adults; progresses to steatohepatitis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma in a subset.
Musculoskeletal disease — even moderate excess weight accelerates knee and hip osteoarthritis; the risk of knee replacement surgery increases 35% for each 5-unit increment in BMI above 25.

Cancer — overweight and obesity are causally linked to at least 13 cancer types including post-menopausal breast, endometrial, colorectal, oesophageal, kidney, and pancreatic cancer, collectively accounting for approximately 4–8% of cancer cases in high-income countries.

supportResources.name: WHO — Obesity and Overweight Fact Sheet
supportResources.description: WHO overview of global overweight and obesity prevalence, causes, health consequences, and policy responses.

supportResources.name: CDC — Healthy Weight, Nutrition, and Physical Activity
supportResources.description: Practical guidance from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention on measuring healthy weight, setting goals, and making lifestyle changes.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Overweight and Obesity
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on overweight assessment, health risks, and treatment from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

sources.title: WHO — Obesity and Overweight Fact Sheet, 2024
Jensen MD et al. 2013 AHA/ACC/TOS Guideline for the Management of Overweight and Obesity in Adults. Circulation, 2014

confusedWithConditions.name: Morbid obesity
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Morbid obesity is defined as a BMI of 40 or above and carries substantially greater risk than overweight (BMI 25–29.9); the same lifestyle measures apply but pharmacotherapy and bariatric surgery are more commonly indicated at that level.

confusedWithConditions.name: Obesity (unspecified)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Obesity begins at a BMI of 30 and above; overweight (E66.3) applies specifically to BMI 25.0–29.9, a range that carries intermediate risk and is typically managed with lifestyle intervention rather than surgery or high-dose pharmacotherapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metabolic syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of cardiometabolic risk factors (central obesity, hypertriglyceridaemia, low HDL, hypertension, hyperglycaemia) that often co-exists with overweight but is a distinct clinical entity; a person can have metabolic syndrome at a normal BMI (metabolically unhealthy normal weight) and vice versa.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Untreated hypothyroidism slows metabolism, causes weight gain, and produces fatigue and cold intolerance that can be mistaken for lifestyle-related overweight; a TSH test distinguishes the two, and thyroid replacement corrects weight gain attributable to hypothyroidism.

relatedConditions.name: Obesity
relatedConditions.relevance: Overweight represents the clinically significant stage before obesity; without intervention, BMI 25–29.9 commonly progresses to BMI above 30, with a marked increase in metabolic and cardiovascular risk.

relatedConditions.name: Metabolic syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Central adiposity at BMI 25–29.9 frequently accompanies metabolic syndrome; treating overweight with lifestyle intervention reduces the prevalence of metabolic syndrome components.

relatedConditions.name: Hypercholesterolaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Excess adiposity raises LDL and triglycerides and lowers HDL; dyslipidaemia and overweight commonly require concurrent management with dietary changes and, when necessary, statin therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnoea
relatedConditions.relevance: Even modest excess weight increases pharyngeal fat and the risk of obstructive sleep apnoea; OSA in turn worsens weight management by disrupting sleep, increasing ghrelin, and impairing adherence to lifestyle programmes.

faqs.question: What BMI is considered overweight?
faqs.answer: A BMI of 25.0 to 29.9 kg/m² is classified as overweight by the World Health Organization for adults. BMI is calculated by dividing weight in kilograms by height in metres squared. Above 30 is obesity. Asian populations face elevated metabolic risk at lower BMI thresholds of 23–24.9.

faqs.question: Is being overweight dangerous?
faqs.answer: Overweight (BMI 25–29.9) raises the risk of type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, obstructive sleep apnoea, joint pain, and several cancers compared with a healthy BMI. A 5–10% weight loss is enough to measurably reduce most of these risks.

faqs.question: How do I know if I am overweight?
faqs.answer: Calculate your BMI by dividing your weight in kilograms by your height in metres squared. A BMI of 25.0–29.9 indicates overweight. Your waist circumference also matters — above 88 cm in women or 94 cm in men indicates central obesity with higher cardiometabolic risk even at a normal BMI.

faqs.question: What causes overweight?
faqs.answer: Overweight results from a sustained imbalance between calories consumed and burned. Key drivers are high intake of ultra-processed foods, physical inactivity, short sleep, stress-related eating, genetic predisposition, and some medications. Secondary hormonal causes like hypothyroidism account for fewer than 5% of cases.

faqs.question: How much weight do I need to lose to improve my health?
faqs.answer: A 5–10% reduction in body weight — for example, 5–8 kg in a 80 kg person — is enough to meaningfully lower blood pressure, blood glucose, and triglycerides and reduce the risk of developing type 2 diabetes by around 50%. You do not need to reach an ideal BMI to benefit.

faqs.question: What is the best diet for losing weight when overweight?
faqs.answer: No single diet is universally best. A Mediterranean-style diet, a low-carbohydrate diet, or any approach that creates a 500 kcal/day deficit all produce similar average weight loss. The most effective diet is one you can sustain; high fibre, adequate protein, and reduced ultra-processed food are common principles across all evidence-based approaches.

faqs.question: Can overweight cause type 2 diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Overweight is the leading modifiable risk factor for type 2 diabetes; the risk roughly doubles for each 5-unit rise in BMI above 22. Losing 5–7% of body weight through lifestyle intervention reduces the risk of developing type 2 diabetes in people with pre-diabetes by approximately 50–60%.

faqs.question: Does overweight cause high blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Excess body weight raises blood pressure through several mechanisms including increased circulating blood volume, activation of the renin-angiotensin system, and sympathetic nervous system overactivation. Approximately 50% of overweight adults have hypertension. Losing even 3–5 kg reduces systolic blood pressure by 3–8 mmHg.

faqs.question: Can medication help with weight loss?
faqs.answer: Yes, for overweight individuals who have not responded to lifestyle change alone and who carry additional metabolic risk factors. GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) produce 10–15% body weight reduction on average. Orlistat and naltrexone-bupropion are alternatives. All are used alongside dietary changes, not instead of them.

faqs.question: How does sleep affect weight?
faqs.answer: Consistently sleeping fewer than 7 hours per night raises ghrelin (which stimulates appetite) and lowers leptin (which signals fullness), increasing daily caloric intake by approximately 300–500 kcal in studies. Poor sleep also raises cortisol, promoting visceral fat deposition. Improving sleep is a recognised weight management strategy.

faqs.question: Is overweight the same as obesity?
faqs.answer: No. Overweight is defined as a BMI of 25.0–29.9 and obesity as 30.0 or above. While both increase health risks, obesity carries substantially higher risk and often warrants more intensive treatment including pharmacotherapy and, for BMI above 35 with comorbidities, bariatric surgery.

faqs.question: Can being overweight cause back and knee pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Each extra kilogram of body weight adds roughly 4 kg of force to the knees during walking. Overweight people have measurably higher rates of osteoarthritis, particularly of the knees and hips. Weight loss of 5–10% reduces joint pain and delays the need for joint replacement surgery.

faqs.question: Does overweight cause fatty liver disease?
faqs.answer: Excess adiposity, particularly visceral fat, drives fat deposition in the liver; non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (now called metabolic-associated steatotic liver disease) is present in approximately 50–75% of overweight and obese individuals. In some it progresses to liver inflammation, scarring, and cirrhosis.

faqs.question: Is overweight linked to cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Overweight and obesity together are causally linked to at least 13 cancer types including post-menopausal breast, endometrial, colorectal, oesophageal, and kidney cancers. The mechanism involves excess oestrogen from adipose tissue, insulin resistance, and chronic inflammation promoting tumour growth.

faqs.question: How does stress cause weight gain?
faqs.answer: Chronic stress raises cortisol, which increases appetite, promotes visceral fat storage, and triggers reward-based eating of energy-dense foods. This pattern operates through dopaminergic brain circuits independently of hunger. Managing stress through mindfulness, cognitive behavioural therapy, or adequate sleep supports weight management.

faqs.question: What is the waist circumference cutoff for health risk?
faqs.answer: For European adults, a waist above 94 cm in men or 80 cm in women indicates increased risk; above 102 cm (men) or 88 cm (women) indicates substantially increased risk. For Asian adults the thresholds are lower — above 90 cm for men and 80 cm for women. Waist circumference predicts metabolic risk better than BMI alone.

faqs.question: Can you be overweight but still healthy?
faqs.answer: Some overweight individuals maintain normal blood pressure, blood glucose, and cholesterol — sometimes called metabolically healthy overweight. However, long-term studies show this phenotype is unstable; most metabolically healthy overweight adults develop at least one metabolic risk factor within 10 years, making early lifestyle intervention worthwhile.

faqs.question: Does quitting smoking cause weight gain?
faqs.answer: Yes, on average. Nicotine suppresses appetite and raises metabolic rate; stopping smoking removes these effects and average weight gain is 4–5 kg in the first year. This should not discourage quitting — the cardiovascular benefits of stopping smoking far outweigh the risk from modest weight gain.

faqs.question: What exercises are best for losing weight when overweight?
faqs.answer: Any exercise burns calories. Brisk walking, cycling, and swimming are low-impact and well tolerated starting points for overweight adults. To lose and maintain weight, aim for 225–300 minutes of moderate activity per week. Adding resistance training preserves muscle mass during caloric restriction and improves insulin sensitivity.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor about being overweight?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if your waist circumference is above the healthy threshold, if you have high blood pressure or blood sugar, if lifestyle changes have not produced results over 3–6 months, or if you suspect a medical cause such as hypothyroidism. A clinician can screen for complications and discuss pharmacotherapy options.

metaTitle: Overweight: Causes, Health Risks & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Overweight (BMI 25–29.9) raises diabetes, heart disease and sleep apnoea risk. Learn causes, waist measurements, diet and treatment options. Doctor-reviewed.
